Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A.

Facts.Text: Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. (PAL) is a Panamanian company incorporated in 1958.
Some time after June 1959, Lasala Investments Limited was renamed North Enterprises Limited (NEL).
Robert Perez De La Sala Sr died in 1967, and Camila Vasquez De La Sala was the sole beneficiary of his will.
By 2005, after Camila Vasquez De La Sala's death, Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala was managing the De La Sala family business interests and assets.
Isabel Brenda Koutsos is a director in each of the Plaintiff Companies, save for JMM.
In August 2011, Ernest instructed ECJ to remit all US dollar deposits held in Singapore to CFC's account with UBS Bank (Canada) Vancouver, which was under Ernest's control.
By August 2011, Ernest had transferred assets worth a total of CAD 663,033,557.61 into his Personal UBS Account.
On 8 August 2011, ECJ passed resolutions to limit Ernest's authority to operate as sole signatory for the accounts of PAL, CFC and DOM with UBS Bank (Canada) Vancouver.
The effect of the resolutions sent Ernest into a rage, who then complained to Isabel who in turn contacted Edward and Christina's father, Bobby.
Edward apologised to Ernest in an email marked 'lo siento mucho' for the role that he had played in passing the resolutions.
After Isabel had spoken to Bobby, ECJ relented and reversed their earlier resolutions.
By then, Ernest had already transferred assets worth a total of CAD 663,033,557.61 into his Personal UBS Account.

On 27 Apr 2011, Laurent Rossier emailed Ernest stating that the amount booked at REC Hasta La Vista was USD 4,125,631.53 plus accrued interest and asking whether Ernest wanted to transfer all funds (including accrued interest) and thereafter close the account.

On 29 April 2011, Ernest signed a letter instructing UBS Trustees (Jersey) Ltd to transfer the net liquidation proceeds, after adding back USD 43,623.01 for fees erroneously charged during the period 2005-2010 for services never rendered, in specie to UBS AG Stamford for UBS Bank Canada, account 101WA165328000, favour #610.630.
As at 1 July 2011, assets valued at CAD 4,035,697.02 were standing to the credit of Ernest's Personal UBS Account.
The REC-Hasta La Vista trust was established by Ernest in October 2004 and was eventually revoked on 25 June 2009.

UBS trustees said that the beneficial ownership of Palomar was different from the beneficial ownership of the trust account, so the money could not go back to Palomar and ended up in Ernest's personal UBS account in Canada.

On 1 March 2005, the assets were transferred to the REC-Hasta La Vista trust from CFC and PAL, including US$700,000 belonging to CFC transferred to UBS AG Singapore in the name of REC-Hasta La Vista Corp.

On 1 March 2005, assets belonging to PAL were transferred to UBS AG Singapore in the name of REC La Vista Corp, comprising US$1,000,000 100% Capital Protected Note 02-17.09.2007 on UBS Currency Portfolio and 1,588.184 units of O'Connor-UBS Currency Portfolio shs J Series 1.
On 6 March 2012, Ernest attempted to transfer US$50m to Isabel, and the transfer failed to go through because of banking issues.
On 30 April 2012, Ernest transferred US$200,000 to Isabel.
On 9 August 2012, Ernest transferred US$250,000 to Isabel.
On 5 December 2012, Ernest transferred US$1m to Isabel.
On 8 January 2013, Ernest transferred US$300,000 to Isabel.
On 17 January 2013, Ernest transferred US$1m to Isabel.

In or around 20 February 2013, the proceeds of sale from 1,700,000 CapitaLand shares, with an approximate value of S$6,791,500 or US$5,489,855 based on the historical average share price on 20 February 2013, were transferred to Isabel.
On 30 September 2011, assets valued at CAD 663,033,557.61 were transferred into Ernest's Personal UBS Account.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company can trace and recover corporate assets that were funneled by a managing director into his personal bank account and subsequently transferred to a family member who also serves as a corporate director.
Party_Details.Rule: A company retains beneficial ownership of its assets even if they are unlawfully diverted into a personal account by a fiduciary. The company may trace the value of these assets into the hands of a third-party recipient. If the recipient receives the assets beneficially and possesses sufficient knowledge of the circumstances—such that retaining the funds would be unconscionable (knowing receipt)—they are strictly liable to restore the funds to the company.
Party_Details.Application: Ernest De La Sala, who managed the family's corporate assets, transferred CAD 663 million belonging to Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and related companies into his personal UBS account. Prior to this, he had also moved approximately CAD 4 million of company assets into the same account via a revoked trust structure. Subsequently, Ernest transferred a total of USD 2,750,000 from this personal account to his sister, Isabel. Isabel was a director of the plaintiff companies and had previously intervened in corporate disputes to protect Ernest's unilateral control over these very accounts. The legal conflict hinges on whether these specific funds transferred to Isabel are legally traceable to the plaintiff companies' assets, and whether Isabel's role and knowledge render her liable for knowing receipt.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the entirety of the funds in Ernest's personal UBS account—both the CAD 663 million and the pre-existing CAD 4 million—were beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies. Consequently, the USD 2,750,000 transferred to Isabel was directly traceable to these misappropriated corporate assets. As there were no legitimate personal funds mixed in the account, complex tracing rules for mixed funds were unnecessary. Regarding knowing receipt, the court rejected Isabel's defense that she was a nominal director ignorant of corporate affairs. Evidence demonstrated she actively intervened in 2011 to protect Ernest's control over the accounts, asserting the funds were 'his' to ward off other family members. Her receipt of millions of dollars, coupled with subsequent formal demand letters from the companies' lawyers informing her of the breach of trust, satisfied the unconscionability standard. Therefore, Isabel was held liable to restore the USD 2,750,000 to the Plaintiff Companies, subject to coordination to prevent double recovery from Ernest.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation [BVI]

Facts.Text: Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation (CFC) is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in 1995.
Isabel Brenda Koutsos is a director of Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, and Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala was a director of Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation at the material time.
On 2005-03-01, US$700,000 belonging to Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation was transferred to UBS AG Singapore in the name of REC-Hasta La Vista Corp as part of the REC-Hasta La Vista trust.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether corporate funds temporarily routed through a family trust structure and ultimately deposited into a director's personal account retain their character as corporate assets, rendering subsequent transfers to third parties recoverable by the company.
Party_Details.Rule: When corporate assets are transferred into a trust or personal account for administrative or tax purposes without the intent to transfer beneficial ownership, the company retains its equitable interest in those funds. The company may trace and recover these assets from any subsequent recipient who takes them with knowledge of the corporate origin.
Party_Details.Application: Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation transferred US$700,000 into the REC-Hasta La Vista trust, an entity established by Ernest De La Sala. Upon the trust's revocation, these funds, alongside other corporate assets, were remitted to Ernest's personal UBS account because the bank required the proceeds to be paid to the settlor. From this pooled personal account, Ernest subsequently transferred USD 2,750,000 to Isabel. The core issue is whether the initial transfer into the trust extinguished Cosmopolitan's beneficial ownership, or whether the funds in Ernest's personal account remained corporate assets subject to tracing.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analyzed the nature of the REC-Hasta La Vista trust and found it was an administrative structure used to hold family corporate assets, not a transfer of beneficial ownership to Ernest personally. The subsequent transfer of the trust's liquidation proceeds into Ernest's personal UBS account was a procedural requirement of the bank, not an assertion of his absolute right to the funds. Therefore, the pre-existing CAD 4 million in his account—which included Cosmopolitan's US$700,000—remained beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies. Because the entire pool of funds in Ernest's account belonged to the companies, the downstream transfers to Isabel were directly traceable to Cosmopolitan and the other plaintiffs. Isabel, possessing the requisite knowledge as a director, was held liable for knowing receipt.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Dominion Corporation S.A.

Facts.Text: Dominion Corporation S.A. (DOM) is a Panamanian company incorporated in 1973.
Dominion Corporation S.A. is owned by Summit Finance Corporation S.A., which in turn is owned by Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A.
In August 2011, members of the De La Sala family, including Ernest, Edward, Christina and James, were directors of Dominion Corporation S.A.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a subsidiary company whose assets were consolidated into a single pool controlled by a fiduciary can trace its beneficial interest into downstream transfers made from that pool to a third party.
Party_Details.Rule: In a corporate group where a fiduciary centrally manages the assets of multiple subsidiaries and aggregates them into a single, unauthorized personal account, each subsidiary retains a beneficial interest in the consolidated pool. The companies may collectively trace their assets into subsequent transfers made from that pool to a recipient liable for knowing receipt.
Party_Details.Application: Dominion Corporation S.A. operates as an indirect subsidiary of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. The assets of Dominion and the other Plaintiff Companies were centrally managed by Ernest and ultimately pooled into his personal UBS Bank (Canada) account, resulting in an aggregate balance exceeding CAD 663 million. Ernest then transferred USD 2,750,000 from this consolidated pool to Isabel. The legal question is whether Dominion, as part of the corporate group whose assets were commingled, can assert a tracing claim against the funds received by Isabel.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Dominion Corporation S.A. was part of the integrated corporate structure used to manage the De La Sala family wealth. Because Ernest pooled the assets of all the Plaintiff Companies—including Dominion—into his personal UBS account, the entire aggregated balance was beneficially owned by the corporate group. The court did not require an exact mathematical apportionment of the specific funds belonging to Dominion versus the other plaintiffs at this stage. Instead, it treated the USD 2,750,000 transferred to Isabel as drawn from the collective corporate pool. Consequently, Isabel was held liable in knowing receipt to the Plaintiff Companies collectively, with the precise internal allocation to be resolved in a subsequent accounting process.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd (JMM) is a Singapore company incorporated in 1948.
John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd is owned by Cambay Prince Steamship Co Ltd (BVI), which in turn is owned by Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited.
ECJ and Ernest are the directors of JMM.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the partial restoration of misappropriated corporate assets into a stake-holding account under the name of a subsidiary precludes the corporate group from pursuing a separate knowing receipt claim against a third party for other diverted funds.
Party_Details.Rule: A company’s right to trace and recover specific misdirected funds from a third-party recipient is an independent cause of action. The partial restoration of separate misappropriated assets into a court-mandated stake-holding account does not extinguish the claim against the third party, provided that the total recovery does not exceed the company's actual loss (the rule against double recovery).
Party_Details.Application: Following litigation against Ernest, USD 250,000,000 was transferred from Ernest's personal accounts to a stake-holding account in the name of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd. However, these transfers did not fully replenish the CAD 663 million originally misappropriated from the corporate group. Concurrently, the companies seek to recover a distinct sum of USD 2,750,000 that Ernest had previously transferred to Isabel. The legal tension is whether the existence of the massive stake-holding account in John Manners' name acts as a bar to recovering the specific funds transferred to Isabel.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the partial replenishment of funds into the John Manners stake-holding account did not fully satisfy the companies' losses, given exchange rate deficits and outstanding claims for lost profits. Therefore, pursuing Isabel for the specific USD 2,750,000 she received did not violate the rule against double recovery. The claim against Isabel for knowing receipt is distinct from the primary recovery efforts against Ernest. The court allowed the claim, stipulating that any funds recovered from Isabel would be factored into the overarching accounting process against Ernest to ensure no double counting occurs.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Peninsula Navigation Company (Private) Limited [BVI]

Facts.Text: Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited (PEN) is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in 1995.

Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited owns all the shares in Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and also owns Straits Marine Company Private Limited and Cambay Prince Steamship Co Ltd (BVI), which in turn owns John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd.
In August 2011, members of the De La Sala family, including Ernest, Edward, Christina and James, were directors of Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a holding company can assert a tracing claim over assets that were aggregated with those of its subsidiaries and diverted into a single unauthorized personal account.
Party_Details.Rule: When a fiduciary improperly consolidates the assets of a holding company and its subsidiaries into a single personal account, the corporate group collectively retains beneficial ownership of the entire pool. The holding company, alongside its subsidiaries, is entitled to trace its interest into downstream transfers made from that pool without needing to perform an exact mathematical apportionment of the funds prior to establishing liability against the recipient.
Party_Details.Application: Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited is the holding company for Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A. and several other subsidiaries. The assets of this entire corporate group were centrally managed by Ernest, who illicitly transferred an aggregated pool of CAD 663 million into his personal UBS account. From this consolidated fund, Ernest transferred USD 2,750,000 to Isabel. The legal issue is whether Peninsula, as the holding entity whose assets were inextricably commingled with its subsidiaries' assets, can validly pursue a knowing receipt claim against Isabel for the transferred funds.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Peninsula was a central part of the corporate structure utilized to hold the De La Sala family wealth. Because Ernest pooled the assets of the entire group—including Peninsula—into his personal account, the court treated the consolidated CAD 663 million as beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies collectively. It was unnecessary to isolate Peninsula's specific financial contribution to the pool to establish liability against the third-party recipient. The court held that the USD 2,750,000 transferred to Isabel was drawn from this collective corporate asset pool, rendering Isabel liable in knowing receipt to the Plaintiff Companies as a group, with internal allocations to be resolved later.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Straits Marine Company Private Limited [BVI]

Facts.Text: Straits Marine Company Private Limited (SMC) is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands in 2008.
Straits Marine Company Private Limited is owned by Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited, and PEN additionally owns SMC.
ECJ, Isabel and Ernest are directors of CFC, PEN, PAL, DOM and SMC.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a subsidiary within a corporate group can jointly pursue a knowing receipt claim when its assets were pooled with those of its sister companies and diverted by a common director.
Party_Details.Rule: Subsidiaries whose assets are improperly aggregated into a single unauthorized account by a fiduciary share a collective beneficial interest in the entire fund. They may jointly trace the value of their assets into subsequent transfers and hold the recipient liable for knowing receipt, with the exact apportionment of damages between the sister companies determined in subsequent accounting.
Party_Details.Application: Straits Marine Company Private Limited, a subsidiary of Peninsula Navigation, had its assets centrally managed by Ernest alongside the other Plaintiff Companies. These assets were consolidated into the CAD 663 million pool transferred to Ernest's personal UBS account. The USD 2,750,000 paid to Isabel was drawn from this exact pool. The issue is whether Straits Marine is a proper plaintiff entitled to trace its interest into the funds received by Isabel.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court recognized Straits Marine as an integral component of the corporate group whose assets were misappropriated and pooled into Ernest's personal account. The court treated the entire account balance as a single pool of assets beneficially owned by the Plaintiff Companies collectively. By establishing that the funds transferred to Isabel originated from this collective pool, the court found Isabel liable in knowing receipt to all the Plaintiff Companies, including Straits Marine, without requiring a precise pre-judgment allocation of the specific funds belonging to each individual entity.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Isabel Brenda Koutsos

Facts.Text: Isabel Brenda Koutsos is a member of the De La Sala family and the sister of Ernest Ferdinand Perez De La Sala.

Isabel Brenda Koutsos was a director of Compañia De Navegación Palomar, S.A., Cosmopolitan Finance Corporation, Dominion Corporation S.A., Peninsula Navigation Company Private Limited and Straits Marine Company Private Limited, and was not a director of John Manners and Co (Malaya) Pte Ltd.

In August 2011, after Ernest informed her about resolutions passed by Edward, Christina and James limiting his authority over certain UBS Bank (Canada) accounts, Isabel contacted their father Bobby to discuss the situation.

In August 2011, Isabel sent an email to Edward stating that Ernest wanted all minutes and communications with UBS Vancouver regarding three companies withdrawn and that Ernest should have sole signatory of all his accounts, describing the funds as his money.
On 2012-03-06, Ernest attempted to transfer USD 50,000,000 from his personal account at UBS Bank (Canada) to Isabel, but the transfer did not complete due to banking issues.
On 2012-04-30, Isabel received a transfer of USD 200,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2012-08-09, Isabel received a transfer of USD 250,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2012-12-05, Isabel received a transfer of USD 1,000,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2013-01-08, Isabel received a transfer of USD 300,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).
On 2013-01-17, Isabel received a transfer of USD 1,000,000 from Ernest's personal account at UBS Bank (Canada).

In or around 2013-02-20, Isabel received the proceeds of sale of 1,700,000 CapitaLand shares from Ernest, with an approximate value of SGD 6,791,500 or USD 5,489,855 based on the historical average share price on that date.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a corporate director who receives millions of dollars from a sibling's personal account is liable for knowing receipt when those funds are traceable to misappropriated company assets and the director actively supported the sibling's absolute control over those accounts.
Party_Details.Rule: A recipient is liable for knowing receipt if they receive assets traceable to a breach of fiduciary duty and possess sufficient knowledge of the circumstances such that retaining the funds is unconscionable. A director cannot claim ignorance of corporate affairs to defeat the knowledge requirement, especially when they actively intervene in corporate governance disputes to protect the fiduciary who misappropriated the funds. The Limitation Act does not impose a time bar on claims to recover trust property from a constructive trustee involved in a fraudulent breach of trust.
Party_Details.Application: A corporate director received multi-million dollar transfers from a sibling's personal account during an escalating family dispute. The dispute concerns whether her status as a director and her awareness of the conflict over corporate control satisfy the knowledge requirement for a knowing receipt claim.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court completely rejected Isabel's defense that she was an ignorant, nominal director. Her active intervention in August 2011 to squash board resolutions and protect Ernest's control over the corporate accounts demonstrated her deep involvement and awareness of the escalating family dispute over the assets. The timing of the transfers—including the failed USD 50 million transfer immediately after litigation commenced against Ernest—further evidenced an orchestrated effort to dissipate assets. As the funds in Ernest's account were entirely corporate assets, tracing was straightforward. Given her role as a director, her active defense of Ernest's unauthorized control, and the explicit legal demands subsequently made upon her, the court found her retention of the USD 2,750,000 to be highly unconscionable. Because the claim involved the recovery of trust property stemming from a fraudulent breach of trust, the statutory time bar did not apply. Isabel was held liable for knowing receipt and ordered to restore the funds.
